enrichment.short_description: All-in-one marketing, sales, service, and CMS platform built around a shared CRM database for growing businesses.
enrichment.seo_title: HubSpot Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: HubSpot review 2026: pricing tiers from free to Enterprise, CRM, Marketing, Sales, and Service Hub features, pros, cons, and top alternatives compared.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: HubSpot is a cloud-based customer platform combining marketing, sales, customer service, content management (CMS), and operations software around a single shared CRM database, so every team works from the same contact and company records instead of siloed tools. It was founded in Cambridge, Massachusetts in 2006 by Brian Halligan and Dharmesh Shah, who met at the MIT Sloan School of Management and built the company around the idea of inbound marketing, the theory that buyers no longer respond to interruptive outbound advertising and instead want to be found through helpful content, SEO, and permission-based engagement. HubSpot went public on the New York Stock Exchange in 2014 under the ticker HUBS and has grown into a large public company headquartered at 2 Canal Park in Cambridge, employing over 10,000 people worldwide with offices across North America, Europe, Asia-Pacific, and Latin America. The product is organized into individual 'Hubs': Marketing Hub (email, landing pages, SEO, automation, ads management), Sales Hub (deal pipelines, sequences, quotes, forecasting), Service Hub (ticketing, knowledge base, customer feedback surveys), CMS Hub (drag-and-drop website building with built-in SEO recommendations), Operations Hub (data sync, workflow automation, data quality tools), and Commerce Hub (invoicing, payments, subscriptions), all of which can be bought individually or bundled into the 'Customer Platform.' Underpinning all of this is HubSpot CRM, which the company made permanently free in 2014 as a wedge product; the free CRM includes contact and deal records, email tracking, meeting scheduling, live chat, and basic reporting for unlimited users. Paid pricing in 2026 scales in three tiers above free: Starter (roughly 15-20 dollars per seat per month billed annually) removes HubSpot branding and adds basic automation and multiple pipelines; Professional (ranging from about 100 dollars per seat per month for Sales/Service Hub up to roughly 890 dollars per month for a 3-seat Marketing Hub Professional bundle) unlocks marketing automation, custom reporting, workflows, and personalization; and Enterprise (from about 150 dollars per seat per month up to 3,600+ dollars per month for bundled platforms) adds custom objects, hierarchical teams, permission sets, sandboxes, and advanced AI features, often layered with a one-time onboarding fee. HubSpot's target audience spans solo founders and small businesses using the free CRM up through mid-market and enterprise revenue teams running complex, multi-hub go-to-market operations, with particularly strong adoption among B2B SaaS, agencies, and services businesses that value inbound marketing and content-led growth. Since roughly 2023 the company has layered generative AI throughout the platform under the Breeze brand, including AI content assistants, a chatbot/agent builder, and predictive lead scoring. Named competitors include Salesforce (more configurable and enterprise-heavy but costlier and more complex to administer), ActiveCampaign and Mailchimp (narrower marketing-automation focus at lower price points), Zoho CRM and Pipedrive (cheaper CRM-first alternatives), and Marketo/Adobe (enterprise marketing automation). HubSpot differentiates primarily through its unified data model across marketing, sales, and service, ease of onboarding relative to Salesforce, and an extensive ecosystem of over 1,700 App Marketplace integrations, agency partners, and educational content through HubSpot Academy.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: HubSpot began as a bet on 'inbound marketing' -- the idea that companies should earn attention through useful content and SEO rather than buying it through interruptive ads. Brian Halligan and Dharmesh Shah founded the company in 2006 out of MIT, and it has since grown from a marketing blog tool into a full customer platform spanning marketing, sales, service, content management, commerce, and operations, all sharing one CRM record for every contact and company.

The platform's core architectural decision -- giving away HubSpot CRM for free and monetizing the automation, reporting, and AI layered on top -- has shaped its go-to-market ever since. Small teams can run entirely on the free tier, while growing companies gradually add paid Hubs as their marketing, sales, or support operations mature, without needing to migrate data between disconnected systems.

HubSpot is publicly traded (NYSE: HUBS) and headquartered in Cambridge, Massachusetts, with a global workforce and a large partner and agency ecosystem that implements and customizes the platform for customers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Marketing Hub covers email marketing, landing pages, SEO recommendations, ad management, and multi-step automation workflows. Sales Hub adds deal pipelines, email sequences, meeting scheduling, quotes, and forecasting, while Service Hub handles ticketing, a knowledge base, live chat, and customer satisfaction surveys -- all built on the same contact timeline so reps and support agents see a complete history of every interaction.

CMS Hub lets marketing teams build and host websites with drag-and-drop modules, adaptive testing, and built-in technical SEO tooling, and Operations Hub keeps third-party data in sync and clean through automation and data quality rules. Across every Hub, HubSpot has added generative AI features under its Breeze branding, including a content assistant, an AI chatbot/agent builder, and predictive lead scoring.

Reporting is centralized in customizable dashboards that pull from marketing, sales, and service data simultaneously, which is one of HubSpot's most cited advantages over stitching together point solutions for each function.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: HubSpot CRM itself remains free for unlimited users, covering contact records, deal tracking, email tracking, forms, and live chat. Paid plans start with Starter (roughly 15-20 dollars per seat per month) which removes HubSpot branding and unlocks basic automation, scale into Professional (100 dollars per seat per month for Sales or Service Hub, or around 890 dollars per month for a 3-seat Marketing Hub Professional bundle) for marketing automation and custom reporting, and top out at Enterprise (150+ dollars per seat per month, or up to roughly 3,600-4,300 dollars per month for bundled Customer Platform editions) for custom objects, advanced permissions, and enterprise AI features.

Costs scale with both seats and contacts, and Professional/Enterprise tiers often carry a mandatory one-time onboarding fee (commonly 3,000 dollars for Marketing Hub Professional). Because pricing compounds quickly across multiple Hubs, most reviewers recommend starting on the free CRM or Starter tier and upgrading only the specific Hub that a team has outgrown.

Unified data model means marketing, sales, and service teams see the same customer history
Strong onboarding, documentation, and HubSpot Academy training resources
Large ecosystem of integrations, templates, and certified agency partners
Regular feature and AI investment keeps the platform competitive
cons: Costs escalate quickly once multiple Hubs and Professional/Enterprise tiers are needed
Onboarding fees on higher tiers add significant upfront cost
Contact-based pricing can penalize businesses with large but low-value contact lists
Advanced customization is less flexible than Salesforce for complex enterprise workflows
Some advanced automation and reporting features are locked behind expensive tiers

faqs.question: Is HubSpot free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. HubSpot CRM is permanently free for unlimited users, including basic contact management, email tracking, forms, and live chat. Paid plans add automation, custom reporting, and higher usage limits.

faqs.question: Who founded HubSpot and when?
faqs.answer: HubSpot was founded in 2006 by Brian Halligan and Dharmesh Shah, who met at the MIT Sloan School of Management and built the company around inbound marketing.

faqs.question: How much does HubSpot cost?
faqs.answer: Pricing ranges from free (CRM) to roughly 20 dollars per seat per month for Starter, 100-890 dollars per month for Professional depending on the Hub, and 150 dollars per seat up to several thousand dollars per month for Enterprise bundles.

faqs.question: What is the difference between HubSpot and HubSpot CRM?
faqs.answer: HubSpot CRM is the free contact and deal management core; 'HubSpot' refers to the full customer platform, which layers paid Marketing, Sales, Service, CMS, Operations, and Commerce Hubs on top of that free CRM.

faqs.question: What are the main HubSpot alternatives?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Salesforce for enterprise-grade CRM customization, ActiveCampaign and Mailchimp for lower-cost marketing automation, and Zoho CRM or Pipedrive for simpler, cheaper sales-focused CRMs.

faqs.question: Is HubSpot a public company?
faqs.answer: Yes. HubSpot has traded on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker HUBS since its 2014 IPO and is headquartered in Cambridge, Massachusetts.

enrichment.short_description: Free, unlimited-user CRM from HubSpot for managing contacts, deals, and pipelines with optional paid upgrades.
enrichment.seo_title: HubSpot CRM Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: HubSpot CRM review 2026: what the free plan includes, Starter/Professional/Enterprise pricing, key features, pros, cons, and top CRM alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: HubSpot CRM is the free customer relationship management product at the core of the broader HubSpot customer platform, built by HubSpot Inc., which Brian Halligan and Dharmesh Shah founded in 2006 in Cambridge, Massachusetts after meeting at the MIT Sloan School of Management. HubSpot launched the CRM as a permanently free product in 2014, deliberately using it as a lead magnet: any business can create contact and company records, log deals through a visual pipeline, track emails, schedule meetings, use live chat and forms, and collaborate with unlimited users at zero cost, with the company instead monetizing premium marketing, sales, and service automation layered on top through paid 'Hubs.' Technically, HubSpot CRM sits on the same underlying object model and database as the rest of the HubSpot platform (Marketing Hub, Sales Hub, Service Hub, CMS Hub, and Operations Hub), which means upgrading later does not require a data migration -- contacts, deals, and activity history carry forward automatically. The free tier supports core CRM functions including a contact and deal timeline, task reminders, basic email templates and tracking, deal and ticket pipelines, and integration with Gmail and Outlook. Paid upgrades unlock progressively more automation and customization: Starter (roughly 15-20 dollars per seat per month) removes HubSpot branding, adds a small number of automated workflows, and increases the number of pipelines; Professional (around 100 dollars per seat per month as part of Sales Hub or Service Hub Professional) adds custom reporting, forecasting, playbooks, and more advanced sequence and automation limits; and Enterprise (from about 150 dollars per seat per month) adds custom objects, advanced permissions, sandboxes, and predictive lead scoring. HubSpot CRM's target audience is broad: solo founders and very small businesses that never need to pay anything, small sales teams that outgrow spreadsheets, and mid-market companies that use the free CRM as the entry point before adopting HubSpot's paid marketing or service tools. HubSpot is a public company (NYSE: HUBS) with more than 10,000 employees worldwide and a large partner ecosystem, giving the free CRM unusually deep documentation, onboarding support, and integration coverage (1,700+ App Marketplace listings) compared to most free CRM tools. Named competitors in the free/entry-level CRM category include Salesforce Starter, Zoho CRM's free tier, Freshsales, Pipedrive, and monday CRM; HubSpot CRM differentiates by pairing genuinely unlimited free users with the option to scale seamlessly into full marketing and service automation without switching vendors, whereas most competing free CRMs cap either users, contacts, or features far more aggressively or require an eventual platform migration to add marketing automation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: HubSpot CRM is the free, foundational product inside HubSpot's broader customer platform. Launched in 2014 as a deliberate lead-generation strategy, it gives unlimited users a shared system of record for contacts, companies, and deals at no cost, with HubSpot instead charging for the marketing, sales, and service automation that can later be layered on top.

Because it runs on the same database as HubSpot's paid Hubs, businesses that start on the free CRM never have to migrate data when they eventually add Marketing Hub, Sales Hub, or Service Hub -- contact history, deal notes, and email tracking carry forward automatically, which is a key differentiator versus free tiers from competing CRM vendors that require a rip-and-replace migration to unlock automation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The free plan includes contact and company records, a visual deal pipeline, task and activity reminders, email tracking and scheduling, meeting scheduling, live chat, and customizable forms, all accessible to unlimited users with no seat limit. A Chrome extension and mobile app let sales reps log activity and track emails from Gmail or Outlook without leaving their inbox.

Paid tiers progressively add automation (Starter), custom reporting, forecasting, and sales sequences (Professional), and custom objects with advanced permissioning and predictive scoring (Enterprise) -- all built as extensions of the same CRM object rather than a separate product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: HubSpot CRM's core functionality -- contact records, deal pipelines, email tracking, and unlimited users -- remains free indefinitely, with no trial expiration. Businesses only start paying when they need HubSpot branding removed, more automated workflows, or additional Sales/Service Hub features.

From there, Starter runs roughly 15-20 dollars per seat per month, Professional (as Sales Hub or Service Hub Professional) runs about 100 dollars per seat per month, and Enterprise starts around 150 dollars per seat per month, all billed per user with annual billing offering a discount over month-to-month.

pros: Genuinely free for unlimited users, not a time-limited trial
No data migration needed when upgrading to paid Hubs
Clean, intuitive interface with a shallow learning curve
Deep integration ecosystem and extensive documentation
Mobile apps and browser extensions support reps working outside the CRM
cons: Free tier has limited automation and customization
Paid tiers get expensive quickly once multiple seats are added
Some reporting and forecasting features require Professional or higher
Contact limits and storage caps can force an upgrade for larger databases
Less configurable than Salesforce for complex, custom sales processes

faqs.question: Is HubSpot CRM really free?
faqs.answer: Yes. The core CRM -- contacts, deals, email tracking, forms, and live chat -- is free indefinitely for unlimited users, not a limited-time trial.

faqs.question: How is HubSpot CRM different from full HubSpot?
faqs.answer: HubSpot CRM is the free contact and deal management foundation. 'HubSpot' more broadly refers to the paid Marketing, Sales, Service, CMS, and Operations Hubs built on top of that same CRM data.

faqs.question: What does HubSpot CRM cost after the free tier?
faqs.answer: Starter begins around 15-20 dollars per seat per month, Professional around 100 dollars per seat per month, and Enterprise from about 150 dollars per seat per month, depending on which Hub is added.

faqs.question: Can I import existing contacts into HubSpot CRM?
faqs.answer: Yes, HubSpot CRM supports CSV imports and native integrations with Gmail, Outlook, and many other tools to bring in existing contact and deal data.

faqs.question: Does HubSpot CRM work on mobile?
faqs.answer: Yes, HubSpot offers iOS and Android apps that let sales reps view records, log calls, and manage tasks from their phone.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to HubSpot CRM?
faqs.answer: Popular free or low-cost alternatives include Zoho CRM, Pipedrive, Freshsales, and monday CRM, though each has different limits on users, contacts, or automation compared to HubSpot's free tier.

enrichment.short_description: Time tracking and workforce management software with screenshots, activity monitoring, GPS, and payroll for remote teams.
enrichment.seo_title: Hubstaff Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Hubstaff review 2026: time tracking, screenshots, GPS, and payroll features, full pricing breakdown, pros, cons, and top workforce management alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Hubstaff is a time tracking and workforce management platform built for companies managing remote, hybrid, or field-based teams, combining automatic time tracking with optional activity monitoring, GPS/geofencing, project budgeting, invoicing, and integrated payroll. It was founded in 2012 in Indianapolis, Indiana by Dave Nevogt and Jared Brown, two entrepreneurs who had been hiring freelancers and remote contractors and could not find software to reliably track hours and productivity across a distributed workforce, so they built Hubstaff to solve their own problem before turning it into a commercial product. The company remains headquartered in Indianapolis and has grown into a widely used tool serving well over 90,000 organizations, from small agencies to distributed enterprises, without taking the large venture-funding path common to many SaaS companies -- Hubstaff has largely bootstrapped its growth. Technically, Hubstaff offers a desktop app (Windows, Mac, Linux), a mobile app (iOS, Android) with GPS tracking, and a Chrome browser extension, all syncing time entries to a central web dashboard in real time. Core functionality includes one-click timers tied to specific projects and tasks, optional and configurable screenshots, keyboard/mouse activity-level tracking, app and URL usage monitoring, GPS location tracking with geofenced job sites that auto-start and stop timers for field teams, project budget tracking against billed time, automatic timesheets with approval workflows, and integrated payroll processing that pays teams directly through PayPal, Wise, Deel, Payoneer, and Gusto. Hubstaff also includes invoicing generated from tracked hours, and integrates with tools like Trello, Asana, QuickBooks, and Slack. Pricing in 2026 runs on a per-seat model with a two-seat minimum on all paid plans: a limited Free plan covers a single user; Starter costs about 7 dollars per seat per month billed monthly (roughly 4.99 dollars billed annually); Grow runs about 9 dollars per seat per month (about 7.50 annually); Team costs about 12 dollars per seat per month (about 10 annually); and Enterprise is priced around 25 dollars per seat per month with expanded reporting, dedicated support, and advanced administrative controls, with annual billing generally discounted to roughly two free months. Hubstaff's target audience spans remote-first startups, digital agencies, outsourcing and staffing firms, and field-service or construction businesses that need GPS-verified time tracking for hourly or distributed workers. Named competitors include Time Doctor and Teramind (similar activity-monitoring and productivity tools), Toggl Track (simpler, monitoring-free time tracking), When I Work and Deputy (scheduling-first workforce tools), and ClickUp/Harvest for teams that want time tracking bundled with broader project management. Hubstaff differentiates through its combination of granular activity/screenshot monitoring with GPS geofencing and native payroll disbursement in one product, which is unusual among lighter-weight time trackers that leave payroll and field-worker location tracking to third-party integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Hubstaff grew out of a personal problem: founders Dave Nevogt and Jared Brown were managing freelance and remote workers in 2012 and couldn't find software that reliably tracked hours and productivity across a distributed team, so they built their own. Based in Indianapolis, the company has since scaled to serve tens of thousands of organizations that need to track time, monitor activity, and pay distributed workers without relying on the honor system.

The product spans desktop, mobile, and browser clients, all feeding a central dashboard that combines timesheets, screenshots, activity levels, GPS data, and payroll in one place -- positioning Hubstaff as much a workforce management suite as a pure time tracker.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Automatic time tracking runs across desktop, mobile, and a Chrome extension, syncing one-click timers to specific projects and tasks in real time. Optional activity monitoring captures screenshots at configurable intervals along with keyboard/mouse activity levels and app or URL usage, giving managers visibility into how tracked time was spent -- a feature that can be disabled per team for organizations that prefer lighter-touch tracking.

For field teams, GPS tracking and geofencing let the mobile app automatically start and stop timers when workers enter or leave a defined job-site radius. Hubstaff also handles the operational side of workforce management: project budgets that track cost against time, automated timesheets with manager approval workflows, client invoicing generated from billed hours, and integrated payroll that pays teams directly through PayPal, Wise, Deel, Payoneer, or Gusto.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Hubstaff prices per seat with a two-seat minimum on every paid plan except Enterprise. Starter costs roughly 7 dollars per seat per month (about 4.99 dollars billed annually), Grow runs about 9 dollars per seat per month (roughly 7.50 annually), and Team costs about 12 dollars per seat per month (roughly 10 dollars annually), each tier unlocking more automation, integrations, and reporting depth.

Enterprise is priced around 25 dollars per seat per month with expanded controls, priority support, and advanced reporting for larger organizations. A limited single-user Free plan exists for individuals, and annual billing across all tiers typically works out to roughly two months free compared to paying monthly.

pros: Combines time tracking, activity monitoring, GPS, and payroll in one product
Flexible monitoring settings let teams choose how much oversight to apply
Native payroll disbursement through multiple payment providers
GPS geofencing is well suited to field and construction teams
Detailed reporting on productivity, budgets, and billable hours
cons: Two-seat minimum on paid plans adds cost for solo users
Screenshot and activity monitoring can feel invasive to employees if not communicated well
Higher tiers needed to unlock GPS, payroll, and advanced reporting
Interface can feel dense for teams that only need basic time tracking
Per-seat pricing scales up quickly for larger teams

faqs.question: Who founded Hubstaff and when?
faqs.answer: Hubstaff was founded in 2012 in Indianapolis, Indiana by Dave Nevogt and Jared Brown, who built it to manage their own remote and freelance workers.

faqs.question: Does Hubstaff have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Hubstaff offers a limited free plan for a single user; paid plans require a minimum of two seats.

faqs.question: How much does Hubstaff cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans range from roughly 7 dollars per seat per month (Starter) up to about 25 dollars per seat per month (Enterprise), with lower rates available on annual billing.

faqs.question: Does Hubstaff take screenshots of employees?
faqs.answer: Screenshot capture is optional and configurable; teams can enable or disable it and set the frequency, and Hubstaff also tracks keyboard/mouse activity levels and app usage as separate optional settings.

faqs.question: Can Hubstaff track field workers with GPS?
faqs.answer: Yes, the mobile app supports GPS tracking and geofencing that can automatically start or stop timers when a worker enters or leaves a defined job-site radius.

faqs.question: Does Hubstaff process payroll?
faqs.answer: Yes, Hubstaff can pay team members directly from approved timesheets through PayPal, Wise, Deel, Payoneer, or Gusto.

use_cases.title: Client billing and project profitability

enrichment.short_description: Free, self-hosted, open-source platform for decentralized social networking, websites, file sharing, and wikis.
enrichment.seo_title: Hubzilla Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Hubzilla review 2026: what this open-source decentralized social platform does, the Zot protocol, self-hosting, pros, cons, and Fediverse alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Hubzilla is a free and open-source set of web applications that run on self-hosted servers called 'hubs,' which connect to each other in a decentralized federated network known as 'the grid.' It traces its lineage to Mistpark, a project Mike Macgirvin released in 2010, which evolved into Friendica; Macgirvin later left Friendica to experiment with a new decentralized identity and access-control framework, forking the codebase in 2012 first under the names Red and Red Matrix before the project was renamed Hubzilla. Macgirvin stepped down as active project coordinator in early 2015, handing management to the open-source community, though he continued contributing periodically and went on to found Zot Labs, the umbrella project home for Hubzilla and its Zot protocol. There is no company behind Hubzilla and no funding history in the traditional sense; it is community-maintained software distributed under the GNU Affero General Public License (AGPLv3), written primarily in PHP and JavaScript, with source code hosted on GitLab and GitHub. The defining technical innovation is the Zot protocol, a JSON-based framework for decentralized identity, communications, and fine-grained, extensible access control across independently operated hubs. Zot enables 'nomadic identity,' meaning a user's channel (their identity, content, and connections) can be cloned across multiple hubs rather than being locked to a single server, so if one hub goes offline or a user wants to migrate, their identity and social graph can move or be mirrored elsewhere without starting over -- a capability most other federated platforms, including Mastodon, do not offer. On top of this identity layer, Hubzilla functions simultaneously as a social network (streams, wall posts, comments, groups), a content management system for building full websites, a file and photo storage/sharing service, a forum and discussion-group host, a wiki and article-publishing system, and a calendar/events tool, all behind a single sign-on that works across every hub in the grid. For interoperability with the broader Fediverse, Hubzilla supports ActivityPub, Diaspora, DFRN, and OStatus protocols, allowing it to federate with Mastodon, Friendica, and other network software beyond hubs running Zot natively. Because Hubzilla is self-hosted, there is no official SaaS pricing; anyone can download and run it for free on their own server or VPS, and the practical cost is whatever hosting a small web server costs, typically a few dollars a month on a low-end VPS. Its target audience is technically inclined users, privacy-conscious communities, and organizations who want an alternative to centralized social platforms and are willing to self-host and administer their own server. Named alternatives and competitors in the federated/decentralized space include Mastodon (simpler, Twitter-like microblogging focus without nomadic identity), Friendica (its sibling project, more social-network-focused with fewer CMS/wiki features), Diaspora (an earlier decentralized social network), Pleroma/Akkoma (lightweight ActivityPub servers), and Nextcloud Social. Hubzilla differentiates from all of them primarily through its breadth -- combining social networking, CMS, file storage, and identity portability in one federated platform -- at the cost of a steeper learning curve and heavier server requirements than single-purpose Fediverse software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Hubzilla is open-source software that individuals or organizations install on their own server, called a 'hub,' which then federates with other independently run hubs to form a decentralized network its community calls 'the grid.' It descends from Mike Macgirvin's earlier project Mistpark and its successor Friendica, forked in 2012 to explore a new decentralized identity system that eventually became the Zot protocol and, ultimately, Hubzilla.

Unlike most social platforms, Hubzilla is not a single service you sign up for -- it is software you (or a hub operator you trust) run yourself, and there is no central company or SaaS offering behind it. Development is community-driven and released under the AGPLv3 open-source license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the core is the Zot protocol, which handles decentralized identity, cross-hub authentication, and fine-grained permissions. This enables 'nomadic identity': a user's channel, including their content, connections, and permissions, can be cloned across multiple hubs, so the account is not permanently tied to any single server the way most federated platforms bind you to your home instance.

Beyond social networking (activity streams, wall posts, groups, private messaging), Hubzilla doubles as a lightweight CMS for building websites, a file and photo storage/sharing tool, a forum and wiki host, and a calendar/events system, all reachable through a single sign-on across every hub a user's identity touches. For federation beyond the Zot-native grid, Hubzilla speaks ActivityPub, Diaspora, DFRN, and OStatus, letting it interoperate with Mastodon and other Fediverse software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Hubzilla itself is free, open-source software with no licensing cost. There is no official hosted plan or subscription tier from a central vendor; instead, users either run their own hub on a VPS or dedicated server, or join a hub operated by a volunteer or community that offers accounts.

The only real cost is server hosting, which for a small hub typically runs a few dollars to a few tens of dollars per month depending on storage, bandwidth, and traffic, plus the time required for a technically capable administrator to install, configure, and maintain the software.

features.title: Zot protocol identity
features.description: Decentralized identity and access-control framework with fine-grained, extensible permissions.

features.title: Nomadic identity
features.description: Clone a channel across multiple hubs so identity and content are not locked to one server.

features.title: Social networking

features.title: Website and CMS tools

cons: Requires self-hosting and technical administration; no simple signup for most users

Smaller, more niche user base than mainstream Fediverse platforms

faqs.question: What is Hubzilla?
faqs.answer: Hubzilla is a free, open-source platform combining social networking, a CMS, file sharing, forums, and wikis, run on self-hosted servers called hubs that federate together into a decentralized network.

faqs.question: Who created Hubzilla?
faqs.answer: Hubzilla descends from Mike Macgirvin's earlier Mistpark and Friendica projects; he forked it in 2012 to build the Zot protocol, which evolved into Hubzilla before he handed project coordination to the community in 2015.

faqs.question: Is Hubzilla free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the software is free and open source under the AGPLv3 license. The only cost is hosting your own server or finding a hub operator willing to host your account.

faqs.question: What is nomadic identity in Hubzilla?
faqs.answer: Nomadic identity, powered by the Zot protocol, lets a user clone their channel, content, and connections across multiple hubs so their identity is not tied to a single server.

faqs.question: Does Hubzilla work with Mastodon?
faqs.answer: Yes, Hubzilla supports the ActivityPub protocol among others, allowing it to federate with Mastodon and other Fediverse platforms.

faqs.question: How is Hubzilla different from Friendica?
faqs.answer: Both share the same creator and lineage, but Hubzilla adds broader CMS, file-sharing, wiki, and nomadic-identity capabilities, while Friendica remains more narrowly focused on social networking.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious community hosting

use_cases.audience: Developers, hobbyists, and small self-hosted communities

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source static site generator written in Go, known for very fast build times and flexible templating.
enrichment.seo_title: Hugo Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Hugo review 2026: how this open-source Go static site generator works, key features, build speed, pros, cons, and top Jamstack alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Hugo is a free, open-source static site generator written in the Go programming language, marketed by its maintainers as 'the world's fastest framework for building websites.' Steve Francia (also known online as spf13) originally created Hugo in 2013 as an open-source project; from version 0.14 in 2015 onward, development has been led by Bjørn Erik Pedersen alongside a broad community of contributors. There is no company behind Hugo and no funding or acquisition history -- it is maintained as a community open-source project, hosted on GitHub under the gohugoio organization, and distributed under the Apache License 2.0. Technically, Hugo takes content written in Markdown, HTML, AsciiDoc, or Org-mode, combines it with data files, templates/layouts, internationalization bundles, static assets, and configuration, and compiles the entire site into static HTML, CSS, and JavaScript files with no server-side runtime required at request time. Because Hugo compiles to a native Go binary rather than running on an interpreted runtime like Ruby (Jekyll) or JavaScript (Gatsby, Eleventy), it is exceptionally fast, capable of building sites with tens of thousands of pages in a few seconds, which is its primary differentiator against most other static site generators. Key features include multilingual/i18n support with per-language content trees, built-in image processing (resizing, cropping, format conversion), asset pipelines for bundling and minifying CSS/JS, custom output formats (so the same content can render as HTML, JSON, RSS, or AMP), Markdown render hooks for customizing how content elements render, shortcodes for embedding reusable snippets inside Markdown, and a live-reloading local development server. Hugo has no built-in CMS or admin UI; content is authored as plain text files in a code repository and typically deployed through static hosts like Netlify, Vercel, GitHub Pages, or Cloudflare Pages, often as part of a Git-based Jamstack workflow, optionally paired with a headless CMS (such as Forestry, Netlify CMS/Decap, or Contentful) for non-technical editors. Notable adopters include Smashing Magazine, which migrated from WordPress to a Hugo-based Jamstack setup in 2017, and Cloudflare, which moved its developer documentation from Gatsby to Hugo in 2022, both citing build speed and simplicity. Hugo's target audience is developers, technical writers, and documentation teams who want a fast, dependency-light way to build blogs, marketing sites, portfolios, and documentation sites without a database or server-side application to maintain. As free, self-hosted software, Hugo has no pricing tiers -- the only costs are optional static hosting (many providers offer generous free tiers) and any headless CMS a team chooses to pair with it. Named competitors include Jekyll (Ruby-based, GitHub Pages' native generator, slower on large sites), Eleventy/11ty (JavaScript-based, minimal and flexible), Gatsby (React-based, more complex with GraphQL data layer), Next.js in static export mode, and Astro (component-island architecture); Hugo differentiates primarily through build speed, a single self-contained binary with no external dependencies to install, and broad built-in functionality (image processing, i18n, asset pipelines) that many competitors require plugins to replicate.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Hugo is an open-source static site generator written in Go, created by Steve Francia in 2013 and led since 2015 by Bjørn Erik Pedersen and a large community of contributors. It compiles Markdown content, templates, and assets into plain static HTML, CSS, and JavaScript with no database or server-side runtime required.

Hugo's headline feature is raw build speed: because it compiles to a native binary in Go rather than running on an interpreted language runtime, it can regenerate sites with tens of thousands of pages in a few seconds, a benchmark it is frequently cited for against Jekyll, Gatsby, and other generators.

As a community-maintained open-source project with no company or funding behind it, Hugo has no official pricing, support contracts, or SaaS offering -- it is distributed as a single self-contained binary under the Apache 2.0 license and installed locally or in CI/CD pipelines.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Hugo supports multilingual sites with per-language content trees and translation bundles, built-in image processing for resizing and format conversion without external tools, and an asset pipeline for bundling, minifying, and fingerprinting CSS and JavaScript. Custom output formats let the same content render as HTML, JSON, RSS, or AMP from one source.

Content authors can use Markdown render hooks to customize how specific elements (links, images, headings) render, and shortcodes to embed reusable components inside otherwise plain Markdown files. A built-in local development server with live reload speeds up the authoring workflow, and Hugo integrates cleanly with Git-based Jamstack deployment pipelines on hosts like Netlify, Vercel, and Cloudflare Pages.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Hugo itself is completely free and open source, with no paid tiers, licenses, or support contracts from a central vendor. Anyone can download the binary and use it for unlimited personal or commercial projects under the Apache 2.0 license.

The practical costs of running a Hugo site come entirely from optional third-party services: static hosting (many providers such as Netlify, Vercel, GitHub Pages, and Cloudflare Pages offer free tiers for typical Hugo sites), a domain name, and an optional headless CMS for non-technical content editors.

features.title: Live-reload dev server
features.description: Local server with instant preview while authoring content and templates.

features.title: No dependencies to install
features.description: Ships as a single self-contained binary with no external runtime required.

pros: Very fast build times even for large sites with thousands of pages
Single binary with no runtime dependencies to install or manage
Extensive built-in features (i18n, image processing, asset pipeline) with no plugins needed
Completely free, open source, and actively maintained by a large community
Deploys easily to any static host or CDN with no server required
cons: No built-in CMS or admin UI, so non-technical editors need a separate headless CMS
Go templating syntax has a steeper learning curve than some competitors
No official commercial support; help comes from community forums and documentation
Less flexible for highly dynamic, component-based UI compared to React-based generators
Some advanced customizations require understanding Go template functions

faqs.question: Is Hugo free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Hugo is completely free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license, with no paid tiers or licensing fees.

faqs.question: Who created Hugo?
faqs.answer: Steve Francia created Hugo in 2013; Bjørn Erik Pedersen has led development since 2015, alongside a large open-source contributor community.

faqs.question: Does Hugo require a database?
faqs.answer: No, Hugo generates static HTML, CSS, and JavaScript files at build time, so deployed sites need no database or server-side application.

faqs.question: What programming language is Hugo written in?
faqs.answer: Hugo is written in Go, which is a key reason it compiles sites significantly faster than interpreted-language generators like Jekyll (Ruby) or Gatsby (JavaScript).

faqs.question: Can Hugo build multilingual websites?
faqs.answer: Yes, Hugo has built-in multilingual support with per-language content trees and translation bundles, no plugins required.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Hugo?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Jekyll, Eleventy (11ty), Gatsby, Astro, and Next.js in static export mode, each with different tradeoffs around speed, templating language, and CMS integration.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source all-in-one project management platform combining issue tracking, docs, chat, and video as a Linear and Notion alternative.
enrichment.seo_title: Huly Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Huly review 2026: open-source project management with issue tracking, chat, and docs, pricing tiers, self-hosting, pros, cons, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Huly is an open-source, all-in-one project management platform built by Hardcore Engineering Inc. as a self-described alternative to Linear, Jira, Slack, Notion, and Motion, aiming to replace several separate work tools with one connected workspace. The platform was founded by Andrey Platov, who serves as CEO, with Hardcore Engineering operating as a small, lean team of roughly a dozen to a few dozen engineers building the platform in the open. Huly combines an issue and task tracker with customizable workflows, a team planner offering a centralized calendar and time-blocking view, a knowledge base and document editor for collaborative writing, built-in instant messaging and group chat channels, and native audio/video conferencing rooms functioning as a 'virtual office,' all inside a single unified data model so switching between a task, a linked document, and a conversation about it does not require leaving the app. Integrations include GitHub (two-way issue and PR sync), Gmail, and Google Calendar, and Huly exposes its data model for further extension since the entire platform is open source. The Huly Platform is released under the commercially permissive EPL-2.0 license, with source code hosted publicly on GitHub under the hcengineering organization; technically it is built on a stack including CockroachDB, Elasticsearch, Redpanda, and MinIO, all containerized and deployable via Docker Compose for self-hosting, with documented minimum requirements of 2 vCPUs and 8 GB RAM (4+ vCPUs and 16 GB RAM recommended for production use). Because the platform is open source, self-hosting is entirely free and unlimited; Hardcore Engineering also historically offered a hosted cloud version, though the company has signaled that free hosted-cloud service is being wound down as it shifts focus, making self-hosting the primary path for teams that want the full platform without relying on the vendor's infrastructure. Where a managed offering is available, pricing follows a tiered structure: a free Common plan for individuals and small teams getting started (10 GB storage, 10 GB video/audio traffic), Rare at roughly 19.99 dollars per month flat rate (100 GB storage and video traffic, unlimited users and unlimited Huly objects), Epic at roughly 99.99 dollars per month for professional teams and small businesses (1 TB storage, 500 GB video traffic), and Legendary at roughly 399.99 dollars per month for large teams needing maximum capacity (10 TB storage, 2 TB video traffic). Huly's target audience is software development teams, startups, and product teams who want the functionality of Linear-style issue tracking plus Notion-style docs plus Slack-style chat without paying for and maintaining four or five separate SaaS subscriptions, as well as privacy- and cost-conscious organizations that prefer to self-host their tools. Named competitors and alternatives include Linear (sleeker, narrower issue tracker only), Jira (enterprise incumbent, heavier and more complex), Notion (docs/wiki-first, weaker native issue tracking), Monday.com and ClickUp (broader work-management suites, closed source), and ChatWoot/Rocket.Chat for the messaging component; Huly differentiates primarily by being fully open source and self-hostable while still covering the breadth of an all-in-one suite, which few of its closed-source competitors offer at any price.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Huly is an open-source project management platform built by Hardcore Engineering Inc., founded by CEO Andrey Platov, designed to replace the combination of Linear, Jira, Notion, Slack, and Motion with a single connected workspace. Rather than being a narrow issue tracker, Huly bundles task management, documents, chat, and video conferencing into one product with a shared data model.

The platform is fully open source under the EPL-2.0 license, and the codebase is publicly maintained on GitHub. Teams can either self-host the full stack via Docker Compose or, where available, use a managed cloud offering, giving organizations flexibility between owning their infrastructure and using a hosted service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Huly's issue tracker supports customizable workflows and statuses similar to Linear or Jira, paired with a team planner that shows a centralized calendar with time-blocking for tasks across a team. A built-in knowledge base and rich-text document editor supports collaborative writing linked directly to issues and projects.

Communication is native rather than bolted on: Huly includes direct and group messaging channels plus audio/video rooms for a 'virtual office' experience, and integrates with GitHub for two-way issue and pull request sync, along with Gmail and Google Calendar connections.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Self-hosting Huly is entirely free since the full platform is open source; the only cost is the infrastructure (recommended 4+ vCPUs and 16 GB RAM) needed to run its CockroachDB, Elasticsearch, Redpanda, and MinIO-based stack via Docker Compose.

Where a managed cloud plan is offered, pricing runs from a free Common tier (10 GB storage/traffic) through Rare (about 19.99 dollars/month, 100 GB), Epic (about 99.99 dollars/month, 1 TB), and Legendary (about 399.99 dollars/month, 10 TB), with every tier including unlimited users and unlimited tracked objects, differing mainly in storage and video/audio traffic allowances rather than per-seat fees.

cons: Self-hosting requires meaningful infrastructure (recommended 16 GB RAM) and DevOps knowledge
Smaller company and community than Linear, Jira, or Notion
Hosted cloud availability has been in flux as the company shifts focus toward self-hosting
UI and feature depth in each module (docs, chat, video) can lag best-in-class single-purpose tools
Documentation and third-party integration ecosystem are less mature than larger competitors

faqs.question: Is Huly free?
faqs.answer: Yes, self-hosting the full open-source platform is free. A hosted cloud version also offers a free Common tier alongside paid Rare, Epic, and Legendary plans.

faqs.question: Who founded Huly?
faqs.answer: Huly is built by Hardcore Engineering Inc., founded by CEO Andrey Platov.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Huly?
faqs.answer: Yes, the full Huly Platform is open source under EPL-2.0 and can be self-hosted via Docker Compose, with recommended minimums of 4+ vCPUs and 16 GB RAM for production use.

faqs.question: What does Huly replace?
faqs.answer: Huly is positioned as an all-in-one alternative to using Linear, Jira, Slack, Notion, and Motion separately, combining issue tracking, docs, chat, and video in one platform.

faqs.question: How much do Huly's paid plans cost?
faqs.answer: Hosted plans run from a free Common tier up to roughly 19.99, 99.99, and 399.99 dollars per month for the Rare, Epic, and Legendary tiers respectively, priced flat rather than per seat.

faqs.question: Does Huly integrate with GitHub?
faqs.answer: Yes, Huly offers native two-way synchronization of issues and pull requests with GitHub repositories.

enrichment.short_description: Modern, AI-powered HRIS for managing employee data, onboarding, time off, and people workflows for growing teams.
enrichment.seo_title: Humaans Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Humaans review 2026: HRIS features, employee data management, onboarding automation, pricing tiers, pros, cons, and top HR software alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Humaans is a modern, cloud-based HR information system (HRIS) built for growing companies that need to centralize employee data and automate people-operations workflows without the complexity of legacy enterprise HR suites. It was founded in London in 2019 (with its funding and public growth phase accelerating from 2020-2021) by Giovanni Luperti, who serves as CEO, and Karolis Narkevicius; Luperti previously worked as Director of Product Design at Qubit before starting Humaans. The company is backed by venture funding, having raised a seed round in March 2021 and a Series A round later in 2021 worth roughly 12-13 million dollars, bringing total disclosed funding to around 20 million dollars across investors including participants in both rounds; Humaans was also part of Y Combinator. The company remains headquartered in London with roughly 40-50 employees. Architecturally, Humaans is built as a flexible, API-first system of record for employee data, emphasizing a consumer-grade user interface (a notable design differentiator versus older HR software) alongside deep configurability: custom fields, custom permission roles, and workflow automation builders let each company model onboarding, offboarding, compensation review, and other HR processes without needing custom development. Core functionality includes a central people directory with document management (storing contracts, IDs, and other employee files), absence and time-off tracking, timesheets, org chart visualization, headcount and compensation management and reporting, real-time analytics and automated reports, and a rewards/benefits marketplace. Onboarding and offboarding are handled through automated, triggerable workflows that assign tasks across HR, IT, and managers. In 2025-2026 Humaans introduced 'Athena,' an agentic AI layer with pre-built AI agents, natural-language workflow building, HRIS/ATS integrations, employee self-service request handling, and intelligent triaging of HR requests and approvals, reflecting a broader push to add AI automation on top of the core HRIS. The platform integrates with Google Workspace, Microsoft 365, Slack, Okta, DocuSign, Greenhouse, and Lattice, among others, and exposes a robust public API for custom integrations. Pricing is tiered: a 'Starting Up' entry plan around 7 dollars per seat per month for smaller teams with core people-data and absence features; a Growth plan (custom-quoted, typically requiring a sales conversation) adding workflow automation, insights/analytics, up to two custom permission roles, and integrations/API access; and an Enterprise plan (custom-quoted) that layers on SAML-based single sign-on including Okta and Entra ID, unlimited custom permission roles and fields, webhooks, audit log export, a dedicated sandbox, a dedicated customer success manager, and an SLA-backed 99.9% uptime guarantee. Humaans' target audience is HR and People teams at fast-growing startups and scale-ups, typically in the 50-1,000 employee range, who have outgrown spreadsheets or a barebones HR tool but do not want the implementation overhead of enterprise suites like Workday or SAP SuccessFactors. Named competitors include BambooHR and Personio (similar mid-market HRIS positioning), HiBob (another design-forward HR platform, particularly strong in Europe), Workday (enterprise-grade but heavier), and Deel/Rippling (broader HR-plus-payroll/IT platforms); Humaans differentiates through its emphasis on interface design, configurability without code, and API-first architecture aimed specifically at fast-moving people teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Humaans is a cloud-based HR information system founded in London in 2019 by CEO Giovanni Luperti and Karolis Narkevicius, aimed at giving growing companies a modern, consumer-grade alternative to legacy HR software. The company went through Y Combinator and raised roughly 20 million dollars across seed and Series A rounds in 2021 to build out the platform.

Rather than trying to be an all-in-one payroll or ATS suite, Humaans positions itself as the flexible system of record for employee data that other HR tools plug into, emphasizing API access, custom fields, and workflow automation so each company can model its own onboarding, offboarding, and compensation processes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core of Humaans is a people directory storing employee records, documents, absence and time-off data, and timesheets, paired with an org chart and headcount/compensation reporting. Automated workflows handle onboarding, offboarding, and compensation review processes, assigning tasks to HR, IT, and managers as employees move through their lifecycle.

Real-time insights and automated reports give People teams visibility into headcount, turnover, and compensation trends, and a rewards marketplace adds employee benefits on top of the core HRIS. More recently, Humaans launched 'Athena,' an agentic AI layer that lets teams build natural-language automations, triage HR requests, and connect pre-built AI agents to existing HRIS and ATS data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Humaans' entry tier, Starting Up, is priced around 7 dollars per seat per month and covers core people-data, document, and absence-tracking features for smaller teams. The Growth plan moves to custom, sales-assisted pricing and adds workflow automation, analytics, limited custom permission roles, and API/integration access.

The top-tier Enterprise plan is also custom-quoted and adds SAML single sign-on, unlimited custom fields and permission roles, webhooks, audit log exports, a dedicated sandbox environment, a named customer success manager, and an uptime SLA, aimed at larger or more security-conscious organizations.

cons: Growth and Enterprise pricing is custom-quoted and not transparent upfront

Not a full payroll or ATS replacement; often used alongside other HR point solutions
Advanced permissioning and SSO are locked behind Enterprise tier
Best suited to companies already comfortable configuring workflows themselves

faqs.question: What is Humaans?
faqs.answer: Humaans is a cloud-based HR information system (HRIS) that centralizes employee data and automates onboarding, offboarding, absence tracking, and other people-operations workflows for growing companies.

faqs.question: Who founded Humaans?
faqs.answer: Humaans was founded in London in 2019 by CEO Giovanni Luperti and Karolis Narkevicius, and went through Y Combinator before raising a seed and Series A round in 2021 totaling roughly 20 million dollars.

faqs.question: How much does Humaans cost?
faqs.answer: The Starting Up plan begins around 7 dollars per seat per month; Growth and Enterprise plans use custom, sales-assisted pricing based on company size and requirements.

faqs.question: Does Humaans handle payroll?
faqs.answer: Humaans is primarily an HR system of record rather than a payroll processor, but it integrates with payroll, ATS, and other HR tools via its API and app integrations.

faqs.question: What is Athena in Humaans?
faqs.answer: Athena is Humaans' agentic AI layer, offering pre-built AI agents, natural-language automation building, and intelligent triaging of employee HR requests.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Humaans?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include BambooHR, Personio, HiBob, Workday, and Rippling, each with different tradeoffs in pricing, configurability, and enterprise readiness.

enrichment.short_description: Not-for-profit event ticketing platform that donates 100 percent of booking-fee profit to charity, mainly education for children.
enrichment.seo_title: Humanitix Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Humanitix review 2026: nonprofit event ticketing features, booking fees, pricing, pros, cons, and how it compares to Eventbrite and other alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Humanitix is an Australian not-for-profit online event ticketing platform built on the premise that the booking fees attendees already pay to ticketing companies could fund charitable causes instead of generating private profit. It was founded in Sydney in 2016 by Adam McCurdie and Joshua Ross, who set out to redirect the same fees charged by conventional ticketing companies like Eventbrite toward social impact rather than shareholder returns. Structured as a not-for-profit organization, Humanitix directs 100 percent of the profit generated from its booking fees to charitable causes, with a primary focus on funding education programs for children in disadvantaged communities in Australia and internationally; by 2026 the platform reports having donated more than 13.8 million dollars to charity since its 2016 founding. In 2026 actor and producer Hugh Jackman joined Humanitix in the role of Head of Impact, adding public profile to the organization's mission-driven positioning. Humanitix has expanded beyond Australia to operate in New Zealand, the United States, and the United Kingdom, with a Sydney headquarters and additional offices in Auckland, Denver, and Edinburgh. Functionally, the platform offers the same core toolset as commercial event ticketing competitors: event creation and a customizable checkout/registration flow, unlimited ticket types for both free and paid events, seating and capacity management, promo codes and discounting, attendee data collection and communication tools, check-in apps for event day, embeddable widgets for organizer websites, and reporting/analytics on sales and attendance, all usable for live shows, conferences, fundraisers, community events, and courses. There are no signup fees and no long-term contracts, and for the majority of event organizers running free events, using Humanitix costs nothing at all. For paid ticketed events, Humanitix charges a booking fee of 5 percent plus 1.29 dollars per paid ticket in the United States (rates vary slightly by country), and by default this fee is passed on to the ticket buyer at checkout, meaning organizers keep 100 percent of their listed ticket price. Registered non-profit organizations and schools qualify for a reduced rate of 3.9 percent plus 1.29 dollars per paid ticket, reflecting the platform's mission-aligned pricing for mission-aligned organizers. Humanitix's target audience spans individual event hosts, community and charity organizers, universities, conference producers, and touring live-events promoters who want full-featured ticketing software but prefer their booking fees to fund social impact rather than enrich a for-profit ticketing company. Named competitors include Eventbrite (the dominant for-profit incumbent with similar feature depth but standard commercial fee structures), Ticketmaster and AXS (for larger venues and touring events), and TryBooking and Humanitix's fellow purpose-driven ticketing platforms; Humanitix differentiates almost entirely on its nonprofit structure and transparent, publicly reported charitable giving, while matching or approaching the feature parity of commercial platforms like Eventbrite on ticketing functionality itself.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Humanitix is a not-for-profit event ticketing platform founded in Sydney, Australia in 2016 by Adam McCurdie and Joshua Ross, built on the idea that the booking fees attendees already pay could fund charitable causes rather than generate private profit for a ticketing company. As a registered not-for-profit, Humanitix directs 100 percent of the profit from its booking fees to charity, with a primary focus on education programs for disadvantaged children.

By 2026 the organization reports having donated more than 13.8 million dollars to charitable causes since launch, and has expanded operations beyond Australia into New Zealand, the United States, and the United Kingdom, with offices in Sydney, Auckland, Denver, and Edinburgh. In 2026, actor Hugh Jackman joined Humanitix as Head of Impact, reflecting the organization's continued push to raise the profile of purpose-driven ticketing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Humanitix offers the standard toolkit expected of a modern ticketing platform: customizable event pages and checkout flows, unlimited ticket types for both free and paid events, seating and capacity management, promo codes, attendee data collection, and a check-in app for event day. Organizers can embed ticketing widgets directly into their own websites and access sales and attendance reporting through a dashboard.

The platform supports a range of event types, including live shows and touring performances, conferences, community and fundraising events, and courses, and requires no signup fee or long-term contract to get started.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: For free events, Humanitix costs organizers nothing at all -- there are no signup fees, subscription costs, or hidden charges. For paid ticketed events, Humanitix charges a booking fee of 5 percent plus 1.29 dollars per paid ticket in the United States, which by default is passed on to the ticket buyer rather than deducted from the organizer's revenue.

Registered non-profits and schools receive a discounted rate of 3.9 percent plus 1.29 dollars per paid ticket. Because 100 percent of the profit from these fees goes to charity, organizers who choose Humanitix are directly funding education and social-impact programs simply by using the platform they would otherwise pay a for-profit ticketing company for.

pros: 100 percent of booking-fee profit goes to charity, mainly children's education programs
No signup fees or long-term contracts for organizers
Discounted rates for registered non-profits and schools
Full-featured ticketing toolkit comparable to commercial platforms
Free events cost organizers nothing to run
cons: Smaller market presence and brand recognition than Eventbrite or Ticketmaster

Fewer large-venue and major touring-event integrations than dominant commercial competitors
Feature set, while solid, is less extensive than long-established enterprise ticketing platforms
Availability of local payment methods and currencies is more limited outside its core markets

faqs.question: What is Humanitix?
faqs.answer: Humanitix is a not-for-profit online event ticketing platform, founded in Sydney in 2016, that donates 100 percent of the profit from its booking fees to charity, primarily education programs for disadvantaged children.

faqs.question: Who founded Humanitix?
faqs.answer: Humanitix was founded in 2016 by Adam McCurdie and Joshua Ross in Sydney, Australia.

faqs.question: How much does Humanitix cost to use?
faqs.answer: It is free to run free events on Humanitix. For paid events, the platform charges a booking fee of 5 percent plus 1.29 dollars per ticket in the US, typically passed on to the buyer, with a discounted 3.9 percent plus 1.29 dollars rate for registered non-profits and schools.

faqs.question: Where does Humanitix's profit go?
faqs.answer: Humanitix directs 100 percent of the profit generated from its booking fees to charitable causes, with a primary focus on education programs for children, having donated over 13.8 million dollars since 2016.

faqs.question: Where does Humanitix operate?
faqs.answer: Humanitix is headquartered in Sydney, Australia, with additional offices in Auckland, Denver, and Edinburgh, and operates in Australia, New Zealand, the United States, and the United Kingdom.

faqs.question: How does Humanitix compare to Eventbrite?
faqs.answer: Humanitix offers comparable ticketing features to Eventbrite but is structured as a not-for-profit that donates its booking-fee profit to charity, whereas Eventbrite is a for-profit commercial platform.

use_cases.title: Community and charity events

use_cases.title: Live shows and touring performances
use_cases.description: Artists and venues sell tickets for concerts and live performances with full checkout and seating tools.

use_cases.title: Conferences and courses
use_cases.description: Organizers manage registration, ticket types, and attendee check-in for professional conferences and educational courses.
